comments.text: A 1989 paper of Burr lists the Ramsey numbers for all graphs with $\leq 6$ edges and no isolated points in a very useful table. Perusing that table shows that indeed $R(G)\leq 18$ for all such graphs $G$, and so the answer to this question is yes for $m=6$.

If not mistaken, for large sizes, the best known bounds on balanced complete bipartite graphs and complete graphs are too weak to deduce the second is larger than the first. It was conjectured that $R(K_{n,n})$ is of the order $2^n$, while the best lower bound for $R(K_t)$ is still like $2^{t/2}$. [https://arxiv.org/pdf/2507.12926: one is maybe not far off to improve $\sqrt 2$ by an epsilon] It may be a nice (but hard) challenge to prove this by pure comparison. As Addenwalla said, $R(P_3)<R(2K_2)$, so the statement fails for the snallest $m$, m=2, where one can compare (m=1 has nothing to do). So the statement is indeed about the behaviour for large sizes...
Judging by how this doesn't obviously fail for $m=6$ and $m=10$, it might be easier to prove the statement when $t=0$. That is, when $H$ is a complete graph.

That is in principle true, but for large values, I think it won't make it that much easier. The Ramsey number for $K_n$ and for the Colex graph $\mathcal C( m)$ where $m=\binom n2+1$ should indeed be the same for large $n$, as in general $R(n,n)>R(n,n-1)+n$ is expected from the exponential behaviour. Intuitively, once one is forced to have a large red $K_n$, there is somewhere else a vertex with many neighbours in that colour as well. If one thinks about hard constructions: for small values, there is a space constraint and the matching $mK_2$ is the one with largest order and thus natural candidate. But this is linear behaviour. For large values, one could think about "rare" structures like the complement of sparse structured constructions, or multipartite graphs like $K_{t,t}, K_{t,t,t}$. If the latter would be worse, the exponential behaviour may have a different base. On the other hand, for the wheel $W_5=K_{2,2,1}$, which has size $8$, $R(W_5)=15<R(K_4) \le R(K_5^{--}).$ Also $26\le R(K_{2,2,2})$ seems to be smaller than $R(K_5)\le 46$. The potential structured counterexamples I thought about, likely won't beat the clique/ Colex graphs in the limit, but even the explicit comparison may be hard.

Hopefully one last comment for now. I see that the problem was updated to indicate that it fails for small $m$. Just to clarify, I claim that it fails for $m\in \{3,4,5,7,8,9\}$ (in particular, I think it is true for $m=6$) and in all of these cases, the reason is $R(mK_2)=3m-1$. From the Radziszowski survey I learned that $R(K_4-e)=10$ and $R(K_5-e)=22$ which is where the $m=4,5,8,9$ cases come from. (The site has been updated to address this comment.)

I lied about that being the last comment... Since the modified conjecture is for $m$ sufficiently large, I don't think that the "falsifiable" designation applies anymore. (The site has been updated to address this comment.)
For completeness, it fails for $m=2$ for the same reason. (The site has been updated to address this comment.)

In my previous comment, I indicated that I couldn't find the statement of this problem in [Er84b]. I still don't see the exact statement, but I now see the following sentence (at the top of page 11) "Probably r(G) is maximal if G is as complete as possible." I guess this must be the intended reference. On the other hand, he makes no reference to Graham, so I continued my search. In https://mathweb.ucsd.edu/~erdosproblems/erdos/newproblems/ExtremeCliques.html, the reference "Some partition theorems in finite graphs" by Erdős and Graham (1973) is given; however, in that paper I was only able to find the statement of the first part (on page 526), not the second part about G having $\binom{n}{2}+t$ edges. I noticed that in https://mathweb.ucsd.edu/~erdosproblems/erdos/newproblems/ExtremeCliques.html, there is an extra condition which says $n\ge 4$. Presumably, this is because $R(3K_2)=8>6=R(K_3)$ (where $mK_2$ is a matching with $m$ edges).* On the other hand, note that $R(6K_2)=17<18=R(K_4)$. Finally I get to my point. I claim that the conjecture is also false for $n=4$ and $t=1$. Let $K_4^{+1}$ be the graph on $5$ vertices and $7$ edges obtained from $K_4$ by adding a new vertex adjacent to one vertex in the $K_4$. I claim that $R(7K_2)=20>18=R(K_4^{+1})$. We already have $18=R(K_4)\leq R(K_4^{+1})$ and to see that $R(K_4^{+1})\leq 18$, the argument is essentially the same as proving that $R(K_4)\leq 18$. Without loss of generality, we can assume that there exists a vertex $x$ and a set $A$ with $|A|=9$ such that every edge from $x$ to $A$ is red. If there is a red triangle in $A$, we are done, so suppose not. If there is a vertex $u\in A$ with 6 blue neighbors in $A$, we are also done (since we will get a blue triangle in the blue neighborhood of $u$). Since it isn't possible for every vertex in $A$ to have blue degree $5$ to $A$, we have a vertex $v\in A$ with red degree at least 4 in $A$ and we can again see that it must be exactly 4 (otherwise we would get a blue $K_5$). Let $B$ be the set of 4 blue neighbors of $v$ in $A$. Every edge in $B$ is blue, and thus every edge from $B$ to $A\setminus B$ is red. So finally, we must have that every edge in $A\setminus B$ is blue in which case we have a blue $K_5$ and we are done. So the conjecture certainly needs to be updated to add the condition that it holds provided the number of edges is large enough. How large is large enough? Can anyone find a counterexample with 8 edges? We have $R(8K_2)=23$ and I wouldn't be surprised if $R(K_4^{+2})\le 22$. However, I am out of time at the moment to attempt to prove this by hand as I did above for $K_4^{+1}$. Conceivably, this could be checked computationally? *In general, Cockyane and Lorimer "The Ramsey number for stripes" (1975) proved that for all positive integers $m$ and $r$, the $r$-color Ramsey number of $mK_2$ is $(r+1)m+(r-1)$. For $r=2$, this says $R(mK_2)=3m-1$ and in this case, the upper bound can be seen by a simple inductive argument (either we have a red edge incident to a blue edge and we can remove those three vertices and apply induction, or else every edge has the same color). The lower bound comes from taking disjoint sets $A$ and $B$ with $m-1$ and $2m-1$ vertices respectively, coloring all edges incident with $A$ red and all other edges blue. (The site has been updated to address this comment.)

Thanks! I realise now that the second question is also implicit in the phrasing "as complete as possible". This is a nice example. Surely some work has been done on this in the past 40 years? It's a natural question. I'll try a deeper search later unless anyone can beat me to it. EDIT: I found nothing yet, but note that Sudakov [Su11] says of this problem in his introduction " This conjecture is very difficult and so far there has been no progress on this problem."

I just checked the Radziszowski "Small Ramsey Numbers" survey and found that surprisingly, $R(K_5-e)=22$. So this means there is a counterexample on both 8 and 9 edges. We have $R(9K_2)=26>22=R(K_5-e)=R(K_4^{+3})$ and $R(8K_2)=23>22=R(K_5-e)\ge R(K_4^{+2})$. Since $R(10K_2)=29<43\leq R(K_5)$, perhaps it is possible that the conjecture holds provided the number of edges is at least 10? I still don't know the exact value of $R(K_4^{+2})$ and it seems too specialized to appear in the Radziszowski survey. So this would still be interesting to know.
In the third paragraph you've written $R(4K_2)=17$, should this be $11$?
Thanks for pointing this out. It should have read $R(6K_2)=17$. I fixed it.
Does anybody see on which page of [Er84b] this problem is mentioned? I scanned through the paper multiple times and couldn't find it. (The site has been updated to address this comment.)

My guess is that Thomas copied the reference from the linked website on problems in Ramsey Theory. On that site, the 1973 paper 'On partition theorems for finite graphs' by Erdős and Graham is also referenced, where they ask the problem at hand on page 526. They also ask another question in their paper, on decomposing $K_{2^n}$. And this latter question can be found in [Er84b]. (The site has been updated to address this comment.)

The first question is mentioned (albeit not as a question) on p.11 of [Er84b]: "Probably $r(G)$ is maximal if $G$ is as complete as possible." It is also asked explicitly as a question on p.526 of this paper of Erdős and Graham. I can't see the second question mentioned in either source; I got it from Chung's collection of Erdős graph problems. (I will update the main problem statement soon.) (The site has been updated to address this comment.)

Thanks both for the reply. These replies came in as I was in the middle of writing my long comment below. So I now think we are all on the same page regarding the two places where the problem is raised.
